title: HTTP
narrativeIntro: The Drift doesn't exist in isolation. There are signals from outside — data sources, APIs, external systems that respond to requests. Angular speaks to them through HTTP: get, post, put, delete. The verbs of the external world. You're about to open a channel.
narrativeOutro: The Drift is connected now. Data flows in from the outside, rendered by your components, managed by your services, navigated by your routes. The system is becoming real.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland sends and receives data from external sources. You learn Angular's HttpClient.
conceptsPreview: HttpClient, Observable, subscribe, loading and error states, HttpErrorResponse

challenges.title: Cold Observable
challenges.narrativeHook: An HTTP call is written. Nobody subscribes. What travels across the network?
challenges.instructions:
An Angular service creates an HTTP GET Observable but the component never calls `.subscribe()` or uses the `async` pipe.

Without running it, predict what happens at the network level.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: No HTTP request is made. Observables are lazy — they do nothing until subscribed.
challenges.predictPrompt: What HTTP request is sent when the component initializes?
predictChoices: No request — Observables are lazy and do nothing until subscribed
A GET request to /api/signals is sent immediately
A GET request is sent but the response is ignored
An error is thrown because nothing subscribed
challenges.predictAnswer: No request — Observables are lazy and do nothing until subscribed

explainGate.prompt: What does it mean for an Observable to be 'lazy', and what two things can trigger HTTP execution? Also, what must be added to app.config.ts before HttpClient can be injected?

hints: Observables are cold (lazy) by default — they represent a potential operation, not an ongoing one.
HttpClient.get() returns an Observable. The HTTP request only fires when something subscribes to it.
Two ways to subscribe: .subscribe() explicitly, or the async pipe in a template.
In a real Angular app, HttpClient must be registered with provideHttpClient() in app.config.ts before it can be injected into any service.

challenges.title: Loading State
challenges.narrativeHook: The request goes out. Something should show while waiting. Something else when it arrives.
challenges.instructions:
Complete `SignalFeedComponent.ngOnInit()` to manage loading state using Angular's Observable pattern.

- Call `this.signalService.getSignals().subscribe({...})`
- `next(data)`: set `this.signals = data`, set `this.loading = false`
- `error(err)`: set `this.error = err.message`, set `this.loading = false`

The mock context at the top simulates Angular's `HttpClient` and `Observable`. In a real app:
- `import { HttpClient } from '@angular/common/http'`
- `import { Observable } from 'rxjs'`
- Add `provideHttpClient()` to `app.config.ts`

files.name: signal-feed.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new SignalFeedComponent(new SignalService(new HttpClient())); if (!c.loading) throw new Error('loading should start as true before ngOnInit');

tests.description: loading should be false after ngOnInit completes
tests.testCode: const c = new SignalFeedComponent(new SignalService(new HttpClient())); c.ngOnInit(); if (c.loading) throw new Error('loading should be false after ngOnInit completes');

tests.description: signals should have 3 items after ngOnInit
tests.testCode: const c = new SignalFeedComponent(new SignalService(new HttpClient())); c.ngOnInit(); if (c.signals.length !== 3) throw new Error(`signals should have 3 items, got ${c.signals.length}`);

tests.description: signals should contain the alpha signal
tests.testCode: const c = new SignalFeedComponent(new SignalService(new HttpClient())); c.ngOnInit(); if (!c.signals.some(s => s.label === 'alpha')) throw new Error('signals should contain { id: 1, label: "alpha" }');

hints: The subscribe method takes an object with next and error callbacks: .subscribe({ next: (data) => { ... }, error: (err) => { ... } })
In the next callback, assign data to this.signals and set this.loading = false.
In the error callback, set this.error = err.message and set this.loading = false. Both callbacks should reset loading.

challenges.title: Missing Subscribe
challenges.narrativeHook: The component calls http.get(). The data never arrives. The Observable was created but never listened to.
challenges.instructions:
The component calls `loadReadings()` in `ngOnInit` but `readings` is always empty.

There is exactly one bug: the Observable returned by `this.http.get()` is never subscribed to. Fix it.

This is the same mistake as forgetting to subscribe in a real Angular service — `http.get('/api/readings')` returns a cold Observable. Nothing happens until you call `.subscribe()`.

files.name: readout.component.ts

tests.description: loadReadings() should populate the readings array
tests.testCode: const c = new ReadoutComponent(new ReadingsService(new HttpClient())); c.loadReadings(); if (c.readings.length === 0) throw new Error('readings is empty — the Observable was not subscribed to');
const c = new ReadoutComponent(new ReadingsService(new HttpClient())); c.loadReadings(); if (c.readings.length !== 4) throw new Error(`readings should have 4 values, got ${c.readings.length}`);
const c = new ReadoutComponent(new ReadingsService(new HttpClient())); c.loadReadings(); if (!c.readings.includes(91)) throw new Error('readings should include 91');

explainGate.prompt: What happens to an Angular HttpClient Observable if you call http.get() but never call .subscribe() on the result?

hints: The Observable returned by http.get() is lazy — calling getReadings() does nothing until you subscribe.
The subscribe callback receives the emitted value — assign it to this.readings inside the callback.

challenges.title: Three-State Fetch
challenges.narrativeHook: Every HTTP call has three possible outcomes: loading, success, failure. Model all three — the way Angular does it.
challenges.instructions:
Complete `DataLoaderComponent.load()` using Angular's Observable subscribe pattern:

1. Set `this.status = 'loading'` immediately
2. Subscribe to `this.service.getSignal()` with a `next` and `error` handler
3. `next(data)`: set `status = 'success'`, store `data` in `result`
4. `error(err)`: set `status = 'error'`, store `err.message` in `errorMsg`

`err` is an `HttpErrorResponse` — Angular's typed error object for HTTP failures. Access its message with `err.message`.

In a real app: `import { HttpClient, HttpErrorResponse } from '@angular/common/http'`

files.name: data-loader.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new DataLoaderComponent(new SignalService(new MockHttpClient(false))); if (c.status !== 'idle') throw new Error(`status should start as 'idle', got '${c.status}'`);

tests.description: load() should set status to 'success' on a successful response
tests.testCode: const c = new DataLoaderComponent(new SignalService(new MockHttpClient(false))); c.load(); if (c.status !== 'success') throw new Error(`status should be 'success', got '${c.status}'`);

tests.description: result should contain the response data on success
tests.testCode: const c = new DataLoaderComponent(new SignalService(new MockHttpClient(false))); c.load(); if (!c.result || c.result.id !== 'XK7') throw new Error(`result should have id 'XK7', got ${JSON.stringify(c.result)}`);

tests.description: load() should set status to 'error' when HttpErrorResponse fires
tests.testCode: const c = new DataLoaderComponent(new SignalService(new MockHttpClient(true))); c.load(); if (c.status !== 'error') throw new Error(`status should be 'error', got '${c.status}'`);

tests.description: errorMsg should contain the HttpErrorResponse message
tests.testCode: const c = new DataLoaderComponent(new SignalService(new MockHttpClient(true))); c.load(); if (c.errorMsg !== 'Connection refused') throw new Error(`errorMsg should be 'Connection refused', got '${c.errorMsg}'`);

hints: Set this.status = 'loading' as the very first line of load(), before subscribing.
Use the object form of subscribe: .subscribe({ next: (data) => { ... }, error: (err) => { ... } })
The error handler receives an HttpErrorResponse object. Access its message with err.message — just like a real Angular HTTP error.
